channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Hot Wheels Infinite Rush is a Child's Imagination Come to Life
item.content:encoded:
My mom would take my brothers and I to the local Toys "R" Us every once in a while, and our go-to was to look for Hot Wheels toys. They were small and extremely cheap, yet provided so much entertainment for their value. I say you can never be too old for Hot Wheels, so if you're looking to relive your childhood, Hot Wheels Infinite Rush might just be your vibe.

During Summer Game Fest Play Days 2026, I had the opportunity to check out the latest Milestone S.r.l. developed title, Hot Wheels Infinite Rush. Known for creating numerous racing titles -- among them Hot Wheels Unleashed and its sequel -- this dev team knows a thing or two about how to make a good car game.

While I expected Hot Wheels Infinite Rush to be similar to their other Hot Wheels titles, in reality, it eschews the genre of pure arcade racer for something more open. I get a sense that Hot Wheels Infinite Rush is more akin to Forza Horizon, with a world filled to the brim with activities.

The final product might have a more specific task for players to accomplish, but for the most part, Hot Wheels Infinite Rush appears to be completely open to tackle in whatever way. With four giant islands to explore, my demo allowed me to explore two of these and go literally anywhere I wanted.

Maps are quite large from my estimation, with the map view obfuscating the true scale of things. Actually driving around showcases an expansive world with tons of other Hot Wheels cars driving around, toy buildings, tracks, and other landmarks to see. The two islands I surveyed were based on a standard American metropolis, and the other was a more rural, rugged, desert-like environment with factories.

In the case of the American city, there's a giant toy ape on top of one of the buildings, so I can only hope that there are multiple ways to interact with landmarks such as this. Hot Wheels Infinite Rush has a mix of more realistic terrain, interspersed with orange tracks, allowing players to defy gravity by driving upside down or hitting loops. 

Scattered around these islands are activities. The most recent example I can think of is like Mario Kart World's open-world mode, where you can explore a large area and drive up to markers highlighting activities of various types. One, for instance, was a demolition derby-type deal that asked me to ram into and destroy as many things as I could within a time limit.

I partook in a few other activities, including some standard races and boss battles against more difficult cars, requiring I hit gates to continue on in the race. Hot Wheels Infinite Rush is a game that leans on the casual side, but that's perfectly fine. It's simple, yet very effective at conveying the feeling of driving a Hot Wheels.

Driving feels speedy and non-convoluted. These are toy cars, after all. Mechanically, you can dash to the side, boost, or drift, but these mechanics are more simplified than those of other racers. Bumping into other cars on the road throws them aside like a child crashing one toy into another. It instills a sense of childhood joy that I thought I could only feel from playing with the actual toys. 

My favorite part about Hot Wheels Infinite Rush is just how many cars there are to choose from. I noticed a few models from my childhood, so there are vehicles from decades ago, as well as ones that are recently released. Milestone promises over 150 different cars, and these can be further customized with stickers and other parts.

Cars are also split into four different classes, each one fitting into some niche, including: Drifters, Versatiles, Speeders, and Titans. These are pretty self-explanatory. Visually, they fit each class, so Titans are larger, bulkier vehicles while Speeders are more like your standard sports car. I used a Titan for the demolition derby I mentioned. This came in handy since Titans are slower and bulkier, making it easy to crash into groups of smaller objects. 

I became a big fan of Milestone after their recent release of Screamer, so I'm more than eager to check out more of their future titles. Hot Wheels games were always ones I meant to check out, and now I have no excuse but to dive into Hot Wheels Infinite Rush when it releases later this year.

Hot Wheels Infinite Rush was played during Summer Game Fest Play Days 2026.

Austin cut his teeth writing various  fan-fiction stories on the RuneScape forums when he was in elementary school. Later on, he developed a deep love… More about Austin



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/hot-wheels-infinite-rush-is-childs-imagination-come-to-life
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Arizona Sunshine Drops the VR for Some Dog-Based Co-Op Action
item.content:encoded:
Arizona Sunshine 2 was one of my first forays into VR gaming. I played it on the PSVR2, a piece of hardware I'm sure you forgot about until just now. Unfortunately, the VR market is in a hard place, and rising hardware costs surely aren't making it any easier. This has led to an interesting pattern recently: VR darlings going flatscreen.

We recently previewed Moss: The Forgotten Relic, a VR-less re-release of Moss that strips away the fourth-wall-breaking interactivity of virtual reality and replaces it all with tried-and-true button presses. Now, the zombie-shooting VR hit Arizona Sunshine is following suit.

During the Future Games Show, Vertigo Games announced the simply named Arizona Sunshine, a re-imagining of the series primarily based on the second VR game. I saw an alpha build of the game during my time in LA for Summer Game Fest and got a behind-the-scenes look at what we can expect for this upcoming VR-less game.

Going Back to Basics

The story is loosely modeled after Arizona Sunshine 2, but the gameplay has been rebuilt from the ground up to be a third-person shooter. That means that a lot of things I took for granted in VR—like having to physically pick up ammo or open containers—all now happen at the press of a button. It's an interesting flip from when I was first trying out VR, where the opposite was true.

The gameplay feels pretty solid and weighty. It's classic third-person shooting action, and you have a massive arsenal of weapons to slaughter zombies with. The up-close melee finishers are satisfying to watch, too. There's nothing about it that felt like a holdover from its VR days—that is to say, nothing here sticks out as weird or off for a third-person shooter.

When I asked the developers why they picked a third-person perspective, they replied that the team had actually tried both third- and first-person gameplay for this new version of Arizona Sunshine. Ultimately, the over-the-shoulder view gave them more opportunity for expression with the game's protagonist, now named Sunny.

In previous games, he didn't really have a name, or at least he never said it during the game (which tracks, considering his absurd crassness). Now, not only does he have a name, but he has a face and whole body to boot—he's not just a floating set of hands and a loud mouth.

Vertigo took this to heart and added all sorts of hats and costumes to the game, letting you dress up and express Sunny how you'd like. Of course, a lot of the options are silly, ranging from a chef's hat to a full-on T-rex costume. This whimsy really shines in the co-op mode, which lets you and a friend take control of two versions of Sunny (the other is in his imagination), and each can dress however they'd like.

However, the arguably more fun experience for player two is playing as Buddy instead. Buddy is Sunny's canine companion, and he's invulnerable to damage from zombies. Additionally, he can squeeze into tight spaces and help solve puzzles. He can take down pretty much any basic zombie with one button, making it a lot of fun to just run around, tackle zombies to the ground, and rip their throats out. Plus, he's a good boy.

This Sunny-and-Buddy co-op experience is great for a duo where player two isn't quite as skilled with first-person shooters. But of course, for the more traditional gamers out there, the classic third-person zombie horde action should suffice. For solo players, you can still issue commands to Buddy, much like the VR version of Arizona Sunshine 2.

With VR out of the way, Vertigo is able to spawn in more zombies per encounter, turning up the heat and pace of Arizona Sunshine. In general, the game is easier to run without VR. The early build I played worked well on a Steam Deck—which bodes well for the planned Nintendo Switch 2 release, as well.

However, the one thing I'm struggling with in this move to non-VR screens was the why for the consumer. I can understand the studio's perspective as it answers to the lack of demand in the VR market, shifting to a more traditional gaming experience.

But arguably, VR was the core ingredient that made Arizona Sunshine special. The novelty of picking up ammo, opening doors, and manually reloading guns really elevated this from the well-trodden zombie-shooting genre.

There's a puzzle early in the VR version of the game where a door stays open only when a big red button is held down. Obviously, you can't be in two places at once, but you can pick up an object and place it on the button. That sounds stupidly easy in reality, sure, but in virtual reality, it felt novel.

That moment is relegated to a non-interactive cutscene in this re-imagining of Arizona Sunshine. Obviously, pressing X to put something on the button wouldn't be satisfying either, but that sort of illustrates my point. Without the novelty of VR, I'm not sure what's really making this game stand out in such a terrifyingly competitive market.

Arizona Sunshine will release on PC, PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, and Nintendo Switch 2, though no release window has been given yet.

Arizona Sunshine was previewed on a laptop and Steam Deck during a preview session with the developer.

Robert Scarpinito is the Features Editor of TechRaptor. With a Bachelor of Arts in Journalism from the Ohio State University, sharing compelling stories is… More about Robert



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/arizona-sunshine-drops-vr-for-some-dog-based-co-op-action
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Ace Combat 8: Wings of Theve Blends Story With High-Octane Dogfighting
item.content:encoded:
Every now and then, a game comes along that tempts me to buy a multihundred dollar flight stick setup. Years ago it was Microsoft Flight Simulator, and now, Ace Combat 8: Wings of Theve is testing my financial responsibility.

I played a short stint of Ace Combat 7, but overall, my experience with flight games is pretty limited. However, Ace Combat 8 is making want to earn my wings with its characters, narrative, and writing pulling me in.

The next game from the Project Aces team puts you in the cockpit with Rex, an ace fighter pilot. Refugees aboard the aircraft carrier Endurance find you adrift at sea, pick you up, and put the weight of an entire fallen nation on your shoulders. No pressure.

A Desperate Flight for Your Nation

In Ace Combat 8, the Federation of Central Usea (FCU) has been invaded by the Republic of Sotoa, and the Endurance is one of the few remaining parts of the FCU's military still going. It's a dire situation, something that only a mythical hero could turn around. That's where the Wings of Theve comes in.

Sometimes, rumors overpower intel in a theater of war. The crew on the Endurance started the myth of the Wings of Theve, a legendary fighter pilot who could give the FCU some hope. That mantle has been bestowed upon you after you witnessed your co-pilot, the original Wings of Theve, die in a crash you survived.

Your copilot was named Cope, and he lives on in your head. He'll come back and monologue pretty often, sounding like a gritty noir detective with a cigarette in one hand and a coffee mug full of whiskey in the other. He's disillusioned with war and fighting, giving you a lot to think about as you fight for the FCU.

It's a level of internal dialogue and storytelling I wasn't expecting from Ace Combat 8, making it more intriguing. An enemy pilot codenamed Shadow 22 is responsible for that fatal crash you survived, and he reappeared in one of the missions I played, too. To me, this feels like a story of personal grudges, national survival, and the power of storytelling, and I'm curious to see where it goes.

Squad-Based Aerial Warfare

Despite how critical the situation is, the in-game banter could easily trick you into thinking that everything is mostly OK in FCU skies. You lead Joker Flight crew with three other pilots. Ellington is the pragmatic, logical type, while Tasha is boisterous, eager, and always looking for a daring excuse to prove herself. Coster rounds out the group with his calm, too-cool-to-talk demeanor—unless we're talking about his favorite rock band.

As you can imagine, these three are a lively crew, with Tasha usually pushing the envelope with risky suggestions while Ellington tries to pull her back. It's ultimately up to you as the squad leader to take action.

You can issue commands using the D-pad, telling your crew to do things like focus on a single enemy or scatter their fire. They do pretty well at taking out the small fry, but don't get mistaken: you're the ace pilot here, and you're the one who'll take down the big targets. Nothing gets done without the Wings of Theve. 

Movement in Ace Combat 8 is blazing fast and hectic, with sudden tight turns and fearless loop-de-loops peppered between bursts of speed. I chased down enemies, locked on some missiles, and let them rip. My jet was equipped with a special payload of cluster missiles that could lock onto four enemies at once, too, and I imagine as you get further, your arsenal only expands.

If I miss an enemy and pass by them, it's easy enough to realign thanks to some of the quality-of-life tools. Holding triangle tracked the enemy, like the ball cam in Rocket League, making it easy to orient yourself toward them. And if you forget which way is up, holding down L1 and R1 levels out your jet. These tools made Ace Combat 8 feel approachable, even for a sortie novice like me.

The spokespeople at Summer Game Fest Play Days encouraged me to skip almost all the cutscenes so I could experience the gameplay. In my one-hour demo, I finished three missions and only picked up snippets of the story from whatever happened in the missions. However, in the few cutscenes I did see, I had full control of the POV, which felt pretty immersive. I'd love to see what else those cutscenes have in store in the full game.

Ace Combat 8: Wings of Theve is slated for an October 2 release on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S.

Ace Combat 8: Wings of Theve was previewed at Summer Game Fest Play Days.

Robert Scarpinito is the Features Editor of TechRaptor. With a Bachelor of Arts in Journalism from the Ohio State University, sharing compelling stories is… More about Robert



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/ace-combat-8-wings-of-theve-blends-story-with-high-octane-dogfighting
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Erosion Is an Open-World Roguelike With a Fresh Take on Meta-Progression
item.content:encoded:
When I played Erosion, I couldn't help but draw immediate comparisons to Enter the Gungeon. The initial gameplay feels pretty similar, and that's not a bad thing—I love Gungeon. The more I played it though, the more I learned how much Erosion really stands on its own.

For one, there's an entire open world here. Sure, your kidnapped daughter awaits you in the Pillar, the game's main dungeon, but you could also go literally anywhere else. Hop on your hoverbike, explore, and find villages, towns, farms, and whatever else.

The catch is: every third time you die, the timeline moves forward by 10 years. This changes a lot in the world, including enemies, towns, and of course, your daughter's age. The ramifications of this aren't clear yet, but the implications are intriguing.

Diving Into the Dungeon

The gameplay feels like a slick twin-stick shooter, and the real fun kicks in once you gather passive power-ups that work toward a build. I started off with a shotgun, which was devastating up close as it spread five bullets. Then I found a power-up that added an extra bullet to the spread. Another made the bullets bounce between enemies, and another extended the bounce's effects even further.

There were other power-ups I simply skipped because I felt like I had a strong build already going. I was clearing rooms, weaving through enemy bullets, and laying down the law like a cowboy should. There were even times when I'd pull out my sidearm, a whip, instead, both to conserve ammo and farm for some more. The whip has a higher chance of dropping ammo, which is a clever survival mechanic.

What really tied the build together was the one active ability I found, which created a clone of my character. That clone would mirror my movements and actions, effectively doubling my shotgun output for a couple of seconds. The downside though, is that the clone could be damaged, effectively doubling my risk of getting hurt. Some risks are worth the reward, so I timed my use of it as best I could.

I cruised through these first few floors. The input feedback was quick, apparent, and satisfying. I always felt in control of my movement, and any time I took a hit, I usually knew it was my fault for not dodging. Sometimes it was hard to tell exactly where the hitbox is and where your invincibility frames are in the dodge roll, though that could just be a byproduct of the game still being in development.

Unfortunately, I died to the boss thanks to its complex bullet pattern. When I respawned on the surface, the developer directed me to instead drive north, away from the entrance to the dungeon. There, I found a farm full of bandits and cleared them out. By exploring more there, I could find permanent powerups, like one that would increase how many passives I could carry in the dungeon.

It's a nice twist on the roguelike formula where permanent meta-progression can be found outside of the "core" gameplay loop. It sounds exciting in the long term, as it'll help keep the gameplay fresh. That abandoned farm could become a thriving community in the future—who's to say?

I imagine the timeline mechanic will also hold more surprises as you spend more time with Erosion. I should note that when I died earlier, I still had two more attempts in this timeline before the world would jump forward by 10 years. So I didn't experience any of the time mechanics. Nonetheless, it holds a lot of intrigue for me. My guess is that more upgrades will become available in the overworld as time continues to progress, though then you run the risk of your character's daughter aging more. Whether that's a bad thing is to be determined.

Erosion has no release window yet, but you can wishlist it on Steam and even request access to the playtest.

Erosion was previewed on a PC at Summer Game Fest Play Days.

Robert Scarpinito is the Features Editor of TechRaptor. With a Bachelor of Arts in Journalism from the Ohio State University, sharing compelling stories is… More about Robert



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/erosion-is-open-world-roguelike-with-fresh-take-on-meta-progression
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Great Indie Games We Saw at Summer Game Fest
item.content:encoded:
If you look at our Summer Game Fest coverage this year, our team saw well over 60 games and went hands-on with the vast majority, ranging from small indie titles to the biggest AAA games coming in the future. Here's some great indie games from a variety of genres we saw at the show.

Bub's Unique Art and Tone Will Stick With You

Developer: Paperfrog | Publisher: Paperfrog

Bub was one of the most interesting games I've seen in a while. Immediately, the style will grab you. Everything on-screen is handmade in some way. That could be cut out construction paper, figures made of dry erase marker on clear sheets, or just about anything you can think of.

What you see on screen changes dramatically from scene to scene, not sticking to one visual medium entirely, to fit the tone of that particular scene. While all are different, there is a cohesion in the style that threads through it all.

The main style is that hand-drawn marker on paper that makes the world feel messy, confused, and somewhat off. It fits the theme of the game as you take on the role of an artist that's sick who is trying to make art of his past memories while he's still able.

Another interesting aspect is the stream of consciousness sort of mumblings of Bub as you walk around. Whether he's making an odd comment on something or you just hear his thoughts when talking to someone, it settles you into this wonderfully weird world.

Bub releases in 2027 on PC.

D-topia is a Charming Dystopian Puzzler

Developer: Marumittu Games | Publisher: Annapurna Interactive

D-topia has a wonderful style that sucks you in with its charm, lightheartedness, and the general upbeat nature of everything going on. An AI has everything sorted to you, all your basic necessities provided to you. What could go wrong?

The backdrop is this dystopian future where you learn more about society and the residents of D-topia. Of course, not is all as it seems, so part of the game is unraveling the mystery of what's going on.

The core gameplay, however, are logic puzzles that are visually simple but so satisfying when everything comes together.

The puzzles I had access to were about moving a set of numbered blocks to the correct spot. There were lines to cross that could increase or decrease the number, sometimes having numbers add up to specific numbers when placed next to another numbered block was important, mechanics where you had to park a square to unblock an area to move a different square, and a whole lot more.

I only saw one type of puzzle, and I know there is more the further you get into the game. If you're a puzzle fan, D-topia is one to keep an eye on.

D-topia releases July 14th on PC, PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, Nintendo Switch, Nintendo Switch 2.

Demi and the Fractured Dream Feels Like a Classic Zelda Game

Developer: Yarn Owl Studios | Publisher: Annapurna Interactive

My brief time with Demi and the Fractured Dream was reminiscent of a classic Zelda game like Ocarina of Time or Windwaker in just a few minutes of playing it.

Unfortunately, my time with Demi wasn't long, but I did get to experience a small snapshot of a dungeon. Zelda vibes jumped out right away with locking on an enemy, though there's a little more action with Demi with its more hack and slash style combat and combos.

One big difference from something like Zelda is the ability to jump. In the boss fight in particular, jumping to miss shockwaves or even the swipe was key to winning.

Environmental puzzles played out simply, such as using a leaf I had to blow wind and uncover something in sand, knocking an object flying through the air towards a target, and even using the leaf on a platform as a way to propel myself around.

Everything about Demi and the Fractured Dream felt like a classic adventure in a wonderfully nostalgic way.

Demi and the Fractured Dream releases February 2027 on PC, PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, Ninteno Switch, and Nintendo Switch 2.

Hoa 2's Hand-Painted Aesthetic Is Gorgeous

Developer: Skrollcat Studio | Publisher: PM Studios

Cute, cozy games are in, and Hoa 2 is looking to cash in on that trend with its lightweight puzzle gameplay and hand-painted art style. You control a little fairy named Hoa (prounounced "hwa") who's trying to revive a robot. Another robot guides the fairy, explaining how to gather Fuel to bring the robot back to life.

Hoa explores the landscape, which feels mostly linear—it's hard to get lost, though sometimes you can skip to the next objective and then have to backtrack. You'll run into cute creatures, like giant crabs or rolypolys, all of which help you platform around the world.

Your main objective lies inside of mini dungeons, where you have to play a game inspired by pipe dream. You press a button to start a stream of Fuel, and is it moves along the pipes, you have to run ahead and rotate joints to get the Fuel to the end. It's simple, if not a little too simple. But for an easygoing game to kick back with and enjoy beautiful visuals, Hoa 2 is a bright and vibrant pick.

Hoa 2 will release in 2026 on PC, PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, and Nintendo Switch 2.

Shot One Fighters is a Great Singleplayer Roguelike Fighting Game

Developer: Red Moon Workshop | Publisher: Red Moon Workshop

Shot One Fighters is such an obviously smart idea I can't believe it's the first time I've seen a game like this. One thing fighting games usually lack is a compelling or long-lasting singleplayer component. Shot One Fighters is the answer.

There are a lot of standard roguelike elements, with a Slay the Spire sort of map to move through for a run. There are even rest points and a shop to buy things at.

You start with a base fighter and one of the rewards after each match may be a new move or a modification to something you have. Think buffs like projectiles do more damage or you can get a new move like a roll.

Each run you build a unique fighter whose combos and playstyle change each time, which is a refreshing way to engage with fighting game mechanics. So much of a fighting game is memorization and muscle memory with certain characters, where Shot One Fighters has an element of that but is more about engaging directly with fighting game mechanics. I'd imagine the more you play, the more well-rounded a player you become.

In any case, I think fighting game fans should keep a close eye on this one.

Shot One Fighters is coming to PC and possibly more in the future. They will be launching a Kickstarter soon, too.

Sonic Pico Park Will Make & Break Friendships

Developer: TECOPARK | Publisher: PICO PARK Inc

Pico Park is a cute co-op 2D puzzle game where each player controls a little guy, and all these little guys have to work together to complete each level. Sonic Pico Park twists the formula a bit by adding four Sonic characters to the mix: Sonic, Tails, Knuckles, and Amy. All four of them can spin dash, which gives a huge burst of speed and can push other characters a bit. On top of that, Tails can fly and carry others under him (though three will weigh him down), and Knuckles can glide across gaps.

Sonic Pico Park is a lighthearted, family-friendly good time. Cooperation is key here; it's impossible to do any puzzle alone, and anyone who tries to be a hero will end up ruining the puzzle for everyone. So when you want to have a relaxed time with some friends (or make some new enemies in the process), keep an eye out for this one.

Sonic Pico Park has no release date, and it will come out on Steam with other platforms to be determined.

Super Yooka-Laylee Kart is a Fun Retro Kart Game

Developer: Playtonic Games | Publisher: Playtonic Friends

Super Yooka-Laylee Kart is a modern refresh of the original Super Mario Kart, which works out great. Largely it still feels like an old school kart game, but there are a lot of modern upgrades.

The first thing is the art style. It is undeniably inspired by something like the original Super Mario Kart, but it has seen an upgrade in a similar way to the HD2D remakes of some classic RPGs. The models and pixel art is there, but so too are reflections on the water, beautiful lighting effects, and more.

As for the racing, it still is just as fast and squirrelly as those old kart games but has more modern elements like drift boosting as well. It's been in development less than a year, so I wouldn't be surprised to see more changed up in the future, too.

One of the biggest features, Super Yooka-Laylee Kart lets you customize races with a ton of options. Those range from adding collision between racers, making racers invisible, making racers small, changing boost power of drifts, making boosts on the tracks slow you down, mirror mode… Let's say the list is long.

Oh, it will also have 8 player local split screen racing. Chaos for sure.

Super Yooka-Laylee Kart has no release date or information on platforms yet.

Valor Mortis Blends Napoleon With Bloodborne

Developer: One More Level | Publisher: One More Level, Lyrical Games

I first saw Valor Mortis at PAX West last year and was absolutely blown away by its atmosphere and combat system. From the developers of Ghostrunner, it blends soulslike elements with weighty first-person combat, and its novel setting in an alternative version of the Napoleonic era hooked me. My demo at Summer Game Fest included a section titled "Chapter 7," and it definitely felt harder compared to the one from PAX.

I only had time to explore the level and couldn't make it to the boss. I ran into a good variety of enemies—one was a short little creature, and another was gigantic and would swing a huge two-handed weapon around. Between them were some average-sized zombified soldiers, some with swords, others with guns.

The combat is intense and visceral; you're up close with these dangerous enemies, having to block, parry, and dodge around them. It's easy to get overwhelmed if you aren't careful. The new weapons I tried were exciting though. The rapier pierces precisely, meaning it can burst weak points for maximum damage (something that previously only guns could do). And the musket felt pretty satisfying to use from afar, doing lethal damage at the cost of a long reload time.

Valor Mortis will release on October 13 (after a fresh delay from the September apocalypse) on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S.



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/great-indie-games-we-saw-at-summer-game-fest
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
